title: Syllabus
text: This course introduces students to quantitative research methods in international relations and European integration using modern tools for scientific programming and reproducible research. Students will learn how to analyze and visualize data in R, document their work using Quarto, and manage their projects efficiently using Git and GitHub. These tools enable students to follow state-of-the-art workflows for transparent, collaborative, and well-organized empirical research. All work will be conducted in Positron, an integrated development environment for data science.

title: Syllabus
text:
Course Objectives
The overall goal of the course is to equip students with the practical skills and methodological confidence necessary to write an empirical bachelor thesis in political science. Students will learn how to preprocess data, select and implement appropriate statistical models for continuous and binary outcomes, compute quantities of interest, and interpret results accurately. Beyond technical proficiency, the course emphasizes sound statistical reasoning, effective data visualization, and the clear communication of empirical findings in a way that is accessible to nontechnical audiences. Through regular assignments and a cumulative final project (take-home exam), students will develop the ability to critically evaluate statistical claims in both academic research and public discourse, providing a solid foundation for conducting their own empirical analyses.

title: Syllabus
text:
Course Logistics

General Information
Each session consists of an instructor-led lecture on concepts and theory, followed by hands-on exercises. Homework is assigned at the end of class and due one week later. Readings provide background, theory, and context for the session. Because homework deadlines are tied to class days, students should remain in their assigned group (Mon or Thu) as their main class. Attending the other day is allowed in case of sickness or other exceptions, but this should remain the exception rather than the norm.


Attendance Policy
Active participation (Mitarbeit) is a required component of the coursework (Studienleistung). This course consists of 13 class sessions. Successful participation is presumed if a student attends at least 80% of class sessions (at least 11 out of 13 sessions).
If attendance falls below 80% but remains at or above 60% (8–10 out of 13 sessions), successful participation is not presumed; in such cases, the possibility of fulfilling the participation requirement will be assessed on a case-by-case basis.
If attendance falls below 60% (fewer than 8 out of 13 sessions), successful participation is normally considered impossible under the examination regulations.
If class sessions are cancelled during the semester, the absolute number of required sessions will be adjusted accordingly while the percentage thresholds (80% and 60%) remain unchanged.


Grading Policy
According to the examination regulations, course assessment consists of coursework (Studienleistung, pass/fail) and a final examination (Prüfungsleistung, graded).
Successful completion of all coursework requirements is a prerequisite for admission to the final take-home exam.

Coursework (Studienleistung, pass/fail)
Coursework consists of two components:
1. Weekly Homework Assignments
Starting in Week 2 (no homework will be assigned in the final week of the semester), students must complete 11 weekly homework assignments. Assignments will be distributed via GitHub and assessed on a pass/fail basis.
To fulfill this requirement, students must pass at least 80% of the assignments (9 out of 11 homeworks). Homework must demonstrate a serious and complete attempt at solving the tasks. Submissions that are empty or clearly insufficient will not be counted as passed.






Note



As homework assignments only started in Week 3, the total number of assignments is 10. Consequently, students must pass at least 8 out of 10 homework assignments to be eligible to take the take-home exam.


2. Active Participation (Mitarbeit)
Active participation, as described in the Attendance Policy, is a required coursework component and will be assessed on a pass/fail basis.
Both components must be passed in order to fulfill the Studienleistung requirement.


Final Examination (Prüfungsleistung, graded)
The final examination consists of a take-home exam, currently scheduled for:
May 28, 19:00 — June 05, 23:59
The exam will be distributed via GitHub. You will receive a dataset and a problem description for a cumulative data analysis project.
You must submit a complete project repository (project folder) via GitHub. The repository must include:

Fully documented and reproducible code

A rendered PDF file presenting your results (structured similarly to a data, methods, and analysis section of a research paper)

All relevant LLM chat histories, if AI tools were used
A statutory declaration according to the University’s guidelines

The version of the repository available on GitHub at the moment the deadline passes will be evaluated.
The take-home exam will be graded according to the university grading scale (1.0 = very good to 5.0 = insufficient) and determines the overall course grade.
All tools and workflows necessary to complete the exam will be taught during the course.



Language and Software Policy
The course is taught entirely in English and focuses on using R, Quarto, and Git/GitHub for data analysis, reproducible research, and project management. Students are encouraged to work with these tools, as the course exercises and workflows are designed around them. For the take-home exam, students may use other script-based languages (e.g., Python or Stata), but this is not recommended, and no guidance will be provided for non-R workflows. The emphasis is on understanding the tools, practicing them, and learning how to independently find solutions.


Hardware
You are required to have a computer available during class. For the purposes of this course, a standard laptop is sufficient; there are no specific requirements regarding RAM, external GPUs, or other advanced hardware components.
If you do not own a suitable device, the University of Mannheim’s IT department offers a laptop rental service. Students are encouraged to make use of this option if needed.


Software
Please install all required software programs and tools following the separate installation guide before the first session, so we can address any potential issues on that day and fully begin the programming component in the subsequent lecture.


Optional Practice Materials
It is highly recommended that you take advantage of the access to DataCamp provided by this course. DataCamp is an online learning platform offering valuable opportunities to practice the material covered in this course and beyond. A good starting point is the Introduction to R tutorial. DataCamp courses are voluntary and not part of the mandatory requirements to pass the course.


Late Work Policy
Late submissions will not be accepted. If you anticipate any difficulties in meeting a deadline, you must discuss this issue with me before the deadline, not after.


AI Policy
All submissions will be checked for plagiarism, and violations of the code of conduct may result in failing the course or expulsion. While generative AI tools, such as ChatGPT or Gemini, are permitted for the final examination, any AI-generated content or code must be clearly highlighted, thoroughly commented, and accompanied by the chat history to demonstrate understanding. I will aim to occasionally provide guidance on using AI responsibly, but relying on it from the start can hinder learning: research shows AI is most effective for those who already understand the topic. For a detailed explanation of why AI can undermine learning, see this explanation by Andrew Heiss.


Mental Health Support
Life and learning at the University of Mannheim can sometimes feel complicated, challenging, or even overwhelming. This is completely normal. You are not expected to fully understand or memorize course content on the first attempt. If you ever feel mentally or emotionally strained, you may find helpful advice on the University of Mannheim’s Good Mental Health and Studying page. Additionally, the Help in Times of Crises page provides an overview of the various counseling and support services available at the university.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Week 3 Goals
By the end of today, you should:

Understand the core concepts: objects, functions, and pipes
Know the main data types R uses to store information
Know the main data structures R uses to organize information
Be able to access and explore data using [] and $

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Agenda

Leftovers from Last Week
Objects, Functions & Pipes
Data Types
Data Structures
Accessing & Exploring Data
Walkthrough & Exercises

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Leftovers from Last Week

Cloning from GitHub

This is how you access and submit the homeworks and take home exam

Creating and linking your private projects with GitHub
Habits for effective and efficient coding:

learn to use shortcuts (e.g. opening and closing Positron panels)
learn to use your keyboard (e.g. highlighting text, adding code cells)
set theme and font that makes you feel good



Budget ~10 minutes

title: QMIR 2026
section: R as a Language for Storing and Transforming Information
text:
R as a Language for Storing and Transforming Information

To understand […] R, two slogans are helpful:
Everything that exists is an object
Everything that happens is a function call
– John Chambers


You give R objects – named containers holding information
You apply functions – operations that transform objects
You chain steps together using pipes


This quote is a good anchor. The point is that R has a very consistent grammar once you understand these three ideas.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Objects – Storing Information


An object has:

a name (how you refer to it)
a value (the information stored)
a type (what kind of information)


An R example:


View source
# Assign a value to a name with <-
country <- "Germany"

gdp_per_capita <- 54300

is_eu_member <- TRUE




The arrow <- is read as “gets”. country gets the value “Germany”. Emphasize that nothing is printed when you assign — you have to call the name to see it.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Objects – Storing Information


View source
# print by typing the name
country


[1] "Germany"


View source
gdp_per_capita


[1] 54300


View source
is_eu_member


[1] TRUE

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Functions – Doing Things with Objects


A function:

takes one or more inputs (arguments)
does something
returns an output

function_name(argument1, argument2, ...)




View source
gdp_values <- c(54300, 43800, 33200)

# Functions transform objects
mean(gdp_values)


[1] 43766.67


View source
sqrt(gdp_per_capita)


[1] 233.0236


View source
nchar(country)


[1] 7

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Functions – Doing Things with Objects


You can write your own functions.
Functions can be anything from simple one-liners to complex software components. Among other things, they can be:

small personal helpers

reusable analysis tools
shared with others

Collections of useful, published functions form packages. We will expore some next week.
Packages are what make R – like Python and many other programming languages – a powerful ecosystem.




View source
# A small custom helper
add_tax <- function(price) {
  price * 1.19
}

add_tax(10)


[1] 11.9

title: QMIR 2026
text: Recap

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Getting help
To open the documentation page of a function,
help(function)
?function()
for example:


View source
help(mean)
?mean()

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Control Structures in R








Structure
Syntax Example
Description




if
if (x > 0) { ... }
Executes code if condition is TRUE


if-else
if (x > 0) { ... } else { ... }
Chooses between two branches


ifelse
ifelse(x > 0, "pos", "neg")
Vectorized conditional


for
for (i in 1:5) { print(i) }
Loop over a sequence


while
while (x < 10) { x <- x + 1 }
Repeat while condition TRUE


repeat
repeat { ...; if (x>10) break }
Infinite loop until break


break
break
Exit a loop immediately


next
next
Skip current iteration in a loop

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Pipe – Chaining Operations
Without the pipe, nested calls become hard to read:


View source
round(mean(sqrt(gdp_values)), digits = 2)


[1] 208.17


With the pipe |>, read left to right – “and then”:


View source
gdp_values |> sqrt() |> mean() |> round(digits = 2)


[1] 208.17




The pipe says: take this, and then do that.


The native pipe |> was introduced in R 4.1. Students may also encounter %>% from the magrittr package — functionally very similar. For this course, |> is preferred.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Comments – Code as Communication


View source
# BAD: comments that just repeat the code
x <- x + 1 # add 1 to x

# GOOD: comments that explain WHY
x <- x + 1 # index starts at 0, shift to 1-based for plotting

# Use comments to:
# - explain decisions ("why log-transform? right skew in raw data")
# - mark sections of your script
# - temporarily disable code during testing




Your most important collaborator is your future self — six months from now, you will not remember why you did what you did. Thanks to our Quarto literate programming workflow, you are living in the golden age of commenting – you can literally write full prose between code cells instead of apologizing in hashtags later.

title: QMIR 2026
section: What Kind of Information Does R Store?
text:
What Kind of Information Does R Store?



Type
Description
Example




double
Decimal numbers
3.14, 54300.5


integer
Whole numbers
42, 2026


character
Text / strings
"Germany", "hello"


logical
True or false
TRUE, FALSE


factor
Categories
"low", "medium", "high"


Date
Calendar dates
2026-02-23

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Numeric: Double & Integer


View source
gdp <- 54300.5 # double (default for numbers)
seats <- 736L # integer (the L suffix makes it explicit)

class(gdp)


[1] "numeric"


View source
class(seats)


[1] "integer"




For most purposes in this course, the integer/double distinction doesn’t matter – R handles it behind the scenes. But it’s good to know it exists.
It is important that you know how to find out if objects are numeric.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Character / String


View source
capital <- 'Berlin' # single or double quotes both work
country <- "Germany" # but double quotes are sometimes easier to see

nchar(country) # how many characters?


[1] 7


View source
toupper(country) # string manipulation


[1] "GERMANY"


View source
paste(country, "is in the EU")


[1] "Germany is in the EU"

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Character / String
Numbers stored as characters are not numbers – R won’t do math with them:


View source
"42" + 1 # this does not work


Error in `"42" + 1`:
! non-numeric argument to binary operator


View source
as.numeric("42") + 1 # transforming the string to numeric makes it work


[1] 43

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Logical / Boolean


View source
is_eu_member <- TRUE

is_eu_member


[1] TRUE


View source
population <- 84000000

# Comparisons produce logical values
population > 50000000


[1] TRUE


View source
population == 84000000


[1] TRUE


View source
!is_eu_member # NOT


[1] FALSE


Logicals are the backbone of filtering – you’ll use them constantly when subsetting data.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Logical Operators








Operator
Meaning
Example




<
Less than
3 < 5


<=
Less than or equal
3 <= 3


>
Greater than
5 > 3


>=
Greater than or equal
5 >= 5


==
Equal to
3 == 3


!=
Not equal to
3 != 5


&
AND (vectorised)
(3 > 1) & (5 > 2)


\|
OR (vectorised)
(3 > 5) \| (5 > 2)


!
NOT
!(3 > 5)


%in%
Is element contained in vector
"Germany" %in% c("France", "Germany")

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Specialized Types: Factors & Dates


Factors – categorical variables with fixed levels


View source
regime <- factor(
  c("democracy", "autocracy", "democracy", "hybrid"),
  levels = c("autocracy", "hybrid", "democracy")
)

regime


[1] democracy autocracy democracy hybrid   
Levels: autocracy hybrid democracy


View source
levels(regime)


[1] "autocracy" "hybrid"    "democracy"



Dates


View source
today <- Sys.Date()
today


[1] "2026-02-22"


View source
treaty <- as.Date("1957-03-25") # Treaty of Rome
treaty


[1] "1957-03-25"


View source
today - treaty # date arithmetic!


Time difference of 25171 days




You’ll encounter these often in real data. The key insight: R treats them differently from plain text so it can do useful things like ordering, arithmetic, and plotting.


Don’t go too deep here. Factors will come up naturally in visualization (Week 4). lubridate for dates can be mentioned as an optional depth topic.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
How Is Information Organized?










Structure
Dimensions
Types allowed
Formal notation
Other names (across fields)




Vector
1
One
\(\mathbf{x}_i\)
feature vector, embedding, signal, series


Matrix
2
One
\(\mathbf{X}_{i_1, i_2}\)
design matrix, adjacency matrix, image, tensor (order 2)


Data frame
2
Multiple

dataset, table, rectangular data, tibble


Array
N
One
\(\mathcal{X}_{i_1 i_2 \dots i_N}\)
tensor, cube, multidimensional array


List
Any
Multiple

container, object, record, nested data

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Vectors – The Building Block


View source
# c() combines values into a vector
countries <- c("Germany", "France", "Italy", "Spain", "Turkey")
population <- c(84, 68, 60, 47, 88) # millions
eu_member <- c(TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE)

length(countries) # number of elements in vector


[1] 5


View source
class(countries) # check type


[1] "character"


View source
class(eu_member) # check type


[1] "logical"

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Vectors – The Building Block
A vector can only hold one type. If you mix types, R silently converts:


View source
mixed <- c(1, 2, "three") # all become character!
mixed


[1] "1"     "2"     "three"

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Matrices & Arrays – Briefly


View source
matrix(1:6, nrow = 2, ncol = 3)


     [,1] [,2] [,3]
[1,]    1    3    5
[2,]    2    4    6


View source
matrix(c("A", "B", "C", "D"), nrow = 2, ncol = 2)


     [,1] [,2]
[1,] "A"  "C" 
[2,] "B"  "D" 



2D structures storing elements of the same type

Fundamental for linear algebra and numerical computations

Data frames are more flexible for tabular data, but matrices remain crucial in statistical modeling


Keep this brief — 2 minutes max. It’s here for conceptual completeness.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Data Frames – The Workhorse



Rectangular (rows × columns)
Each column is a vector (one type)
Columns can be different types from each other
Each row is an observation




View source
eu_data <- data.frame(
  country = c("Germany", "France", "Italy", "Spain", "Turkey"),
  population = c(84, 68, 60, 47, 88),
  eu_member = c(TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE),
  regime = c("democracy", "democracy", "democracy", "democracy", "hybrid")
)

eu_data


  country population eu_member    regime
1 Germany         84      TRUE democracy
2  France         68      TRUE democracy
3   Italy         60      TRUE democracy
4   Spain         47      TRUE democracy
5  Turkey         88     FALSE    hybrid

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Data Frames – The Workhorse




View source
nrow(eu_data) # number of rows (observations)


[1] 5


View source
ncol(eu_data) # number of columns (variables)


[1] 4


View source
head(eu_data) # first 5 rows of df


  country population eu_member    regime
1 Germany         84      TRUE democracy
2  France         68      TRUE democracy
3   Italy         60      TRUE democracy
4   Spain         47      TRUE democracy
5  Turkey         88     FALSE    hybrid





View source
str(eu_data)


'data.frame':   5 obs. of  4 variables:
 $ country   : chr  "Germany" "France" "Italy" "Spain" ...
 $ population: num  84 68 60 47 88
 $ eu_member : logi  TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE FALSE
 $ regime    : chr  "democracy" "democracy" "democracy" "democracy" ...


View source
summary(eu_data)


   country            population   eu_member          regime         
 Length:5           Min.   :47.0   Mode :logical   Length:5          
 Class :character   1st Qu.:60.0   FALSE:1         Class :character  
 Mode  :character   Median :68.0   TRUE :4         Mode  :character  
                    Mean   :69.4

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Lists – The Flexible Container


View source
country_info <- list(
  name = "Germany",
  population = 84000000,
  eu_member = TRUE,
  parties = c("SPD", "CDU", "Greens", "FDP")
)

country_info


$name
[1] "Germany"

$population
[1] 8.4e+07

$eu_member
[1] TRUE

$parties
[1] "SPD"    "CDU"    "Greens" "FDP"   


Lists can hold anything – including other lists. They’re less intuitive than data frames, but you’ll encounter them constantly as outputs of statistical models.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Reaching Inside Structures
Two main tools:

[ ] – index by position or condition
$ – index by name (for data frames and lists)

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Indexing Vectors with [ ]




View source
countries <- c("Germany", "France", "Italy", "Spain", "Poland")

countries[1] # first element


[1] "Germany"


View source
countries[c(1, 3)] # first and third


[1] "Germany" "Italy"  


View source
countries[-2] # everything except second


[1] "Germany" "Italy"   "Spain"   "Poland" 





View source
population <- c(84, 68, 60, 47, 38)

# Logical indexing -- filter by condition
countries[population > 50]


[1] "Germany" "France"  "Italy"

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Indexing DFs with [ ] and $




View source
# [row, column] -- leave blank to mean "all"
eu_data[1, ] # first row, all columns


  country population eu_member    regime
1 Germany         84      TRUE democracy


View source
eu_data[, 2] # all rows, second column


[1] 84 68 60 47 88


View source
eu_data[1, 2] # specific cell


[1] 84




View source
# $ accesses a column by name -- returns a vector
eu_data$country


[1] "Germany" "France"  "Italy"   "Spain"   "Turkey" 


View source
eu_data$population


[1] 84 68 60 47 88





View source
# Combine: filter rows by condition
eu_data[eu_data$population > 65, ]


  country population eu_member    regime
1 Germany         84      TRUE democracy
2  France         68      TRUE democracy
5  Turkey         88     FALSE    hybrid

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Walkthrough Overview

Clone the Week 3 exercise repo from GitHub (same workflow as last week)
Open exercise_week03.qmd in Positron
Work through the tasks in order
Fast finishers: optional depth exercises at the bottom of the document


Live-code the clone step briefly to warm students up. Then let them work. Circulate and help. Reserve ~10 min at the end for questions before previewing homework.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Exercise Structure
Core tasks (everyone):

Create vectors of different types and check their class
Build a small data frame from scratch – use a political science example
Subset with [ ] and $
Write clean, commented code throughout

Optional exercises:

Working with factors: level ordering and table()
Date arithmetic with as.Date()

Optional advanced exercises:

if-conditions and for-loops

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Homework

Distributed via GitHub (same workflow as Week 2)
Submit a rendered Quarto document (.qmd + rendered PDF)
Due: one week from today

The homework follows the same arc as today – you’ll work with a small political dataset to practice types, structures, and subsetting. Read the task descriptions carefully and write comments explaining your reasoning.

title: QMIR 2026

title: QMIR 2026
text:
What you will learn

How to build a reproducible, open-source research workflow – integrating data, code, analysis, and writing into a transparent scientific process
How to use statistical models to learn from data – understanding what models assume, what they estimate, how they update our beliefs, and how to interpret results responsibly
How to communicate empirical results clearly and responsibly – through well-structured reports, visualizations, and reproducible documents

title: QMIR 2026
section: What this course does not cover
text:
What this course does not cover

Data collection and research design – we work with existing datasets and do not cover survey design, measurement strategies, or fieldwork (the university offers dedicated courses and resources in these areas)
Causal inference methods – we focus on analyzing and interpreting associations between variables rather than formal causal identification (specialized courses cover causal inference in depth)
Substantive political theory or normative argumentation – we use real political science examples, but the focus is methodological and conceptual training rather than developing theoretical frameworks

title: Getting Started: Installation Guide
text: This course introduces you to a range of software tools which, taken together, form a powerful and contemporary toolbox for transparent, reproducible, and scalable empirical research. Rather than relying on a single piece of software, you will learn how different tools interact and complement one another — much like they do in real-world research workflows.

title: Getting Started: Installation Guide
text:
Configure Git
To use Git, you need to set a name and email address. These will be associated with every change (commit) you make, acting like a digital signature. You do not need to use your full name, but it is recommended.
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your@mail.com"

title: Getting Started: Installation Guide
text:
Create a GitHub account
If you don’t already have one, create a free GitHub account here.
GitHub will allow you to store repositories online, collaborate with others, and track your work from anywhere. We will use GitHub to distribute course materials.

title: QMIR 2026
text: What is Reproducible Research?

title: QMIR 2026
text:
What is Reproducible Research?



Reproducible research means:

Every results can be regenerated from raw data
Every transformation and coding decision is documented
Every table and figure can be recreated automatically
Analytical choices are transparent and traceable




Why this matters:

Reviewers can understand and verify your analysis
Coauthors can follow and extend your work
You can easily test alternative specifications (e.g., add variable X)
Your future self knows exactly what you did
If your laptop dies tomorrow, nothing is lost

GitHub \(\rightarrow\) enables remote storage and collaboration ☁️🤝

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Output embedded in documents


## Example of embedded figure

```r
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| label: fig-demo
#| fig-cap: "A demo figure"
library(forcats)
library(ggplot2)

demo_data <- data.frame(
  country = c("Germany", "France", "Italy", "Spain", "Poland"),
  pop = c(83, 67, 60, 47, 38)
)

ggplot(
  data = demo_data,
```

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Version Control with Git

Repository (repo) \(\rightarrow\) project folder with memory 🗂️
git init \(\rightarrow\) Turns a normal folder into a Git repository 🚀
Stage (git add) \(\rightarrow\) Select changes you want to include in the next snapshot 📦
Commit \(\rightarrow\) A saved snapshot of staged changes 📸
Commit message \(\rightarrow\) Short explanation of what and why you changed something 💬
History (git log) \(\rightarrow\) Timeline of all committed snapshots 🕰️
Remote \(\rightarrow\) Online copy of your repository (e.g., GitHub) ☁️

Git & GitHub – A Folder Structure Reminder
A few students ran into problems with last week’s homework. Here is a summary of how to set things up correctly.
One dedicated QMIR folder, at a stable path on your computer:
Documents/
  QMIR_2026/  ← your top-level course folder. Individual repos go INSIDE here, e.g:
    hw-w04/ 
    lab-w04/
    ...

✅ Place individual repositories inside the QMIR folder
❌ Do not turn the top-level QMIR_2026/ folder itself into a Git repo — Git repos inside Git repos cause problems
❌ Do not place your QMIR folder inside a cloud-synced folder (iCloud Drive, Google Drive, OneDrive, Dropbox) — syncing services and Git conflict with each other and corrupt repositories

section: The Tibble – A Better Data Frame
text:
The Tibble – A Better Data Frame


The tidyverse uses tibbles instead of base R data frames.
They are the same idea – rows × columns, each column one type – but with better defaults:

Prints only the first 10 rows
Shows column types below the header
Never converts strings to factors silently
Never creates row names

title: QMIR 2026
text:
What is Tidy Data?

Tidy datasets are all alike, but every messy dataset is messy in its own way.
– Hadley Wickham


A dataset is tidy when:

Each variable has its own column
Each observation has its own row
Each value has its own cell

title: QMIR 2026
section: Tidy vs. Wide Data – An Example
text:
Tidy vs. Wide Data – An Example
Wide format (common in spreadsheets, not tidy):



country
gdpPercap_1952
gdpPercap_1957
gdpPercap_1962




Germany
7144
10188
12902


France
7030
8663
10560




Long (tidy) format (what the tidyverse expects):



country
year
gdpPercap




Germany
1952
7144


Germany
1957
10188


France
1952
7030


France
1957
8663






In the tidy version, year is a variable – a column. In the wide version, year is hiding inside column names. The tidyverse (and especially ggplot2) expects tidy data.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Reshaping with tidyr – A Brief Preview
text:
Reshaping with tidyr – A Brief Preview
You will encounter wide data in the wild. tidyr provides two functions to convert between formats:

pivot_longer() – wide → long (tidy)
pivot_wider() – long → wide



View source
# Hypothetical wide dataset
wide_data |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("gdpPercap_"),
    names_to = "year",
    values_to = "gdpPercap"
  )



We won’t go deep on reshaping today – the gapminder data is already tidy. But knowing these functions exist will save you hours when you start working with real-world data.

The Data Science Cycle


Everything we do in this course fits somewhere in this cycle:
Import → Tidy → Transform → Visualise → Model → Communicate
Where we are today:

✅ Import – readr
✅ Tidy – we got lucky, gapminder is already tidy
✅ Transform – dplyr
👀 Visualise – ggplot2 preview
📅 Model – coming later in the course
✅ Communicate – Quarto, all along





Source: Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel, and Grolemund (2023)

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Installing and Loading Packages
Before you can use a package, you need to:

Install it once (downloads from CRAN to your computer)
Load it in each session (makes its functions available)



View source
# Install once -- you only need to do this one time
install.packages("gapminder")
install.packages("tidyverse")

# Load every session -- goes at the top of your .qmd
library(tidyverse)
library(gapminder)









Tip


Never put install.packages() inside a Quarto document that you intend to render or share. Quarto blocks package installation during rendering for security reasons, so the document will fail to render and throw an error. In addition, even if it were allowed, it would attempt to reinstall the package every time the document is rendered, which can create unnecessary delays and compatibility issues in other users’ environments.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Data Import with readr
readr is the tidyverse package for importing rectangular data files.



Function
File type




read_csv("file.csv")
Comma-separated values


read_tsv("file.tsv")
Tab-separated values


read_delim("file.txt", delim = ";")
Any delimiter






View source
# Import a CSV file and assign it to an object
my_data <- read_csv("data/my_dataset.csv")




For other formats:


View source
library(haven)
my_data <- read_dta("data/dataset.dta") # Stata
my_data <- read_sav("data/dataset.sav") # SPSS

library(readxl)
my_data <- read_excel("data/dataset.xlsx") # Excel





Always assign the result to an object. read_csv("file.csv") alone reads the file but immediately discards it.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Data Export
To save a data frame to disk:


View source
# Write to CSV
write_csv(my_data, "output/my_dataset_clean.csv")

# Write to RDS (R's native format -- preserves types exactly)
saveRDS(my_data, "output/my_dataset_clean.rds")

# Read it back
my_data <- readRDS("output/my_dataset_clean.rds")











Format
Pros
Cons




.csv
Universal, opens in Excel
Loses type info (factors become strings)


.rds
Preserves all R types exactly
Only readable in R


.dta
Stata-compatible
Requires haven

title: QMIR 2026
section: dplyr – A Grammar of Data Manipulation
text:
dplyr – A Grammar of Data Manipulation


All dplyr functions follow the same pattern:

First argument: the data frame (automatically filled if working with pipe |>)
Subsequent arguments: what to do
Return value: always a (new) data frame


The core verbs:



Verb
What it does




filter()
Keep rows that meet a condition


select()
Keep or drop columns


mutate()
Create or modify variables


arrange()
Sort rows


summarise()
Compute summary statistics


group_by()
Group rows for grouped operations


ungroup()
Remove grouping

title: QMIR 2026
section: Putting It Together – A Full Pipeline

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Bad Practice vs. Good Practice


Bad practice – nested or intermediate objects:


View source
gap_2007 <- filter(gapminder, year == 2007)
gap_gdp <- mutate(gap_2007, gdp = pop * gdpPercap)
gap_grp <- group_by(gap_gdp, continent)
summarise(gap_grp, mean_gdp = mean(gdp))



Good practice – piped chain:


View source
gapminder |>
  filter(year == 2007) |>
  mutate(gdp = pop * gdpPercap) |>
  group_by(continent) |>
  summarise(mean_gdp = mean(gdp))




Both produce the same result. The piped version reads like a sentence and requires no intermediate objects. However, you should always be aware of what each individual pipe step does. Especially when learning, test each step separately in the console to make sure you understand how the data changes at every stage.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Grammar of Graphics
ggplot2 builds plots from layers. Every plot starts the same way:
ggplot(data = <DATA>, aes(x = <VAR>, y = <VAR>, ...)) +
  geom_*()

The three essential pieces:

ggplot() – sets up the canvas and declares the data
aes() – maps variables to visual properties (x, y, colour, size, …)
geom_*() – the geometric object that draws the data (points, lines, bars, …)




Note the + operator, not the pipe |>. ggplot2 layers are added with +.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
A First Scatterplot




View source
gapminder |>
  filter(year == 2007) |>
  ggplot(aes(
    x = gdpPercap,
    y = lifeExp,
    color = continent
  )) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    x = "GDP per Capita (USD)",
    y = "Life Expectancy (years)",
    color = "Continent"
  )








Figure 1: GDP per capita vs. life expectancy, 2007






The plot in Figure 1 shows the classic wealth-health relationship: richer countries tend to have longer life expectancies.
Notice:

data piped into ggplot()
aes() maps variables to visual properties
geom_point() adds the points layer
theme_bw() gives a clean look
labs() adds axis labels

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Log Scale and facet_wrap()




View source
gapminder |>
  filter(year == 2007) |>
  ggplot(aes(
    x = gdpPercap,
    y = lifeExp
  )) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  facet_wrap(~continent) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    x = "GDP per Capita (log scale)",
    y = "Life Expectancy"
  )








Figure 2: GDP vs. life expectancy by continent, 2007






scale_x_log10() – log-transforms the x axis, making the skewed GDP distribution easier to read.
facet_wrap(~ continent) – creates one small panel per continent.
We will go much deeper on ggplot2 next week. Today you are just seeing the shape of it.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Execution Options – Global vs. Cell Level
text:
Execution Options – Global vs. Cell Level
Last week you saw echo and eval as cell-level options. You can also set them globally in the YAML header so they apply to all cells at once:
execute:
  eval: true    # run all code cells
  echo: true    # show all source code
  warning: false  # suppress warnings (e.g. tidyverse startup)
  message: false  # suppress messages
  error: false  # stop on errors (set true to render despite errors)
  freeze: true  # don't re-run cells unless source changes

Cell-level options override global ones for that specific cell:
```{r}
#| echo: false   # overrides global echo: true for this cell only
my_plot
```

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Cell Options Reference








Option
Values
What it controls




eval
true / false
Whether the code is executed


echo
true / false
Whether the source code is shown


warning
true / false
Whether warnings appear in output


message
true / false
Whether messages appear in output


error
true / false
Whether errors stop rendering


include
true / false
Whether cell output appears at all


label
fig-* / tbl-* / any string
Cell identifier for cross-referencing


fig-cap
string
Caption for figure output

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Labels and Cross-References
Labelling a code cell that produces a figure enables automatic cross-references in your prose:


```{r}
#| label: fig-lifeexp
#| fig-cap: "Life expectancy over time"
#| message: false

gapminder |>
  group_by(year, continent) |>
  summarise(mean_le = mean(lifeExp),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = mean_le,
             color = continent)) +
  geom_line() +
  theme_bw()
```

As shown in @fig-lifeexp, life
expectancy has risen across all
continents since 1952.


The label must start with fig- for figures, tbl- for tables
@fig-lifeexp in prose becomes “Figure 1” (auto-numbered) in the rendered output
Figure numbers update automatically if you add or reorder figures
Works in HTML, PDF, and Word output

This is how academic papers keep figure references consistent. Your future self will thank you.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Suppressing Tidyverse Messages
When you load tidyverse, R prints a message listing attached packages. When you use read_csv(), it prints column types. These are useful interactively, but clutter your rendered document.

Suppress them with cell options or globally:


Cell level:
```{r}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
library(tidyverse)
library(gapminder)
```

Global YAML (applies to all cells):
execute:
  message: false
  warning: false



Prefer the global approach in homework submissions so your rendered PDF looks clean.
